english: wág* (wag) Ts.) a band of ten soldiers. — bcù-gcig-zál (bcu gcig zhal) the eleven-faced (Awalokiteswara) Glr.

from the phrases: sems kón-med-pa daṅ bcùgs med-pa daṅ γnód-pa med-pa (sems kon med pa dang bcugs med pa dang gnod pa med pa) Stg., and Pratihārya Avadāna (v. Feer) p. 3: lha-byin-gyis bcùgs byás-te (lha byin gyis bcugs byas te) = देवदत्तविगृहीतेन, it appears, that bcùgs (bcugs) signifies hatred, hostility, damage, loss, which when compared with γcùgs (gcugs) seems rather strange, yet is in accordance with कौतस (for this must probably be read inst. of कोक्कत).

(रस) moisture, juice, sap, but gen. combined with the notion of a certain inherent virtue or power; zlá-bai bcùd (zla ba'i bcud) a fructifying moisture, to be compared in its effects to the warmth of the sun, and prob. means night-dew (if after all it is any thing real); hence essence, nutriment, rkáṅ-gis bcùd g̥yur (rkang gis bcud 'gyur) nourishment comes from the marrow Med.; bcùd-la soṅ (bcud la song), Mil. also bcùd-la bor (bcud la bor), (this food) has proved a nutritious fluid, it agrees with him; bcùd-càn (bcud can) nutritious, succulent, of grass, food etc.; bcùd-méd (bcud med) not nutritious, Med.; invigorating cordial, quintessence, bcùd-lén (bcud len) an elixir of life; frq. fig.: cǒs ťams-càd bsdús-pai bcùd (chos thams cad bsdus pa'i bcud) Glr.
1. v. j̥úm-pa ('jum pa). — 2. to use artifices, to chicane Sch.

1. to be flattened down Sch. — 2. Kun. *lúṅ-po čúr-te yoṅ* (lung po cur te yong) there is a draught (here). — 3. C. like bkág-pa (bkag pa) to bar, obstruct, block up, e.g. of snow obstructing a road. Cf. j̥úr-ba ('jur ba).

1. to heap or pile up Cs.; Lex.: šiṅ pùṅ-por bcér-ba (shing pung por bcer ba) to pile up wood. — 2. = bcìr-ba (bcir ba) 1. to squeeze, to press C., W.; to squeeze in, ri-brág γnyis-kyi bár du (ri brag gnyis kyi bar du) something between two rocks Pth.; *cèr táṅ-cè* (cer tang ce) W. to squeeze, press, screw in; *cèr-cér táṅ-cè* (cer cer tang ce) W. to throng, to crowd.
for bcù (bcu) in bcò-lṅá (bco lnga) 15, and bcò-brgyád (bco brgyad) 18; lo lṅa γsum bcò-lṅá (lo lnga gsum bco lnga) 3 times 5, 15 years (lṅa γsum (lnga gsum) standing pleon.) Mil.

pf. and imp. bcòs (bcos), prop. root of the fut. tense of c̥ós-pa ('chos pa), but in W. the usual word for byéd-pa (byed pa) to make, perform; to prepare, manufacture, construct; employed in all kinds of phrases; *Kó-la zún-càn cò* (kho la rdzun can co) W. (he) makes him a liar.

pf. of j̥óms-pa ('joms pa), conquered, subdued; having conquered or subdued, e.g. dgrá-bcòms-pa (dgra bcoms pa), v. dgra (dgra); victory Cs.; p̥ŕog-bcóm ('phrog bcom), and *còm-tóg* (com tog) W. robbery and acts of violence. — bcòm-brlág (bcom brlag) p.n., Mathura, town of ancient India, in the neighbourhood of Agra, Zam., Tar. — bcòm-ldán (bcom ldan) victorious Cs.; bcòm-ldan-d̥ás (bcom ldan 'das) (Kh. *wcòm-ldan-dé* (wcom ldan de), Ld. *còm-dan-dás* (com dan das), C. *còm-dän-dä* (com dan da)) भगवन्त Cs.: victorious, Sch.: 'the victoriously consummated', Burn. le bien-heureux, the usual epithet of Buddha, Burn. I., 71.
v. c̥ól-ba ('chol ba); bcól-ma (bcol ma) a thing committed to a person's charge, a trust.

a verb of its own, though as to form resembling a participle, 1. to treat medically, hence to cure, to heal, mkás kyaṅ bcòs-su med (mkhas kyang bcos su med) he cannot be cured even by the best physicians Med.; bcòs-(pai) ťabs (bcos pa'i thabs) the way of treating, the method of curing Med.; sman-bcǒs (sman bcos) medical treatment Med. — 2. to do (a thing) for the sake of appearance, for form's sake, to affect, bcòs-su byéd-pa (bcos su byed pa) to perform a sham work, e.g. blowing into a blazing fire C.; hence as sbst.: a false conception, wrong idea, bcós pa daṅ k̥rúl-bar gyúr-ba (bcos pa dang 'khrul bar gyur ba) to give way to odd fancies, to have crotchets in the brain, e.g. in consequence of old age Thgy. — 3. partic.: made or contrived by art,
